coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent biosensor based on graphene quantum dots/zirconium-based metal-organic framework nanocomposite as a peroxidase mimic for cholesterol monitoring in human serum
coredata.dc:creator: Abdolmohammad-Zadeh, Hossein
Ahmadian, Farzaneh
coredata.dc:description: In this study, graphene quantum dots/zirconium-based metal–organic framework (GQDs/UiO-66) nanocomposite was synthesized via a solvothermal procedure in one pot. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite in peroxidase-like reactions was investigated using colorimetric and spectrofluorometric techniques. The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite in peroxidase mimetic-based fluorescence analysis of H2O2 based on H2O2-terephthalic acid reaction was considerably enhanced in comparison with its components. Owing to the fact that H2O2 could be produced as a result of enzymatic oxidation of cholesterol, and there is a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and cholesterol concentration, a fluorescent biosensor was developed for indirect determination of the cholesterol. In the optimal condition, the calibration plot was linear in the concentration range between 0.04 and 1.60 µmol L−1, with a detection limit of 0.01 µmol L−1. The method validation was checked by analyzing the NIST SRM 1952a-2, as a certified reference material, and the evolved technique was successfully used for cholesterol quantification in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cholesterol
Metal-organic framework
Graphene quantum dots
Nanocomposite
Peroxidase-like activity
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of the GQDs/UiO-66
H2O2 detection using GQDs/UiO-66 as a peroxidase mimetic
Applicability of the method for cholesterol sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of the research council of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant no. ASMU/99372-20) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of UiO-66 (a), GQD (b) and GQD/UiO-66 (c) and TEM images of UiO-66 (d), GQD (e) and GQD/UiO-66.

(A) XRD spectra of UiO-66 (a), GQD (b) and GQD/UiO-66 (c), (B) FT-IR spectra of (a) GQD, (b) UiO-66 and GQD/UiO-66 (c), and (C) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherme, and pore size distribution curve (inset) of the synthesized GQD/UiO-66 nanocomposite.
The fluorescence spectra of (a) TA/GQD/UiO-66 (blank), (b) TA/H2O2, (c) TA/H2O2/UiO-66 (d) TA/H2O2/GQD, and (e) TA/H2O2/GQD/UiO-66.

Influence of (a) pH, (b) buffer type and its concentration, (c) concentration of TA, (d) incubation (irradiation) time, and (e) concentration of GQD/UiO-66 nanocomposite and the weight ratio of its component on the analytical signal. The error bars are representative of the standard deviation of three measurement repetitions.
Michaelies- Menten constant (Km) and maximum reaction rate (Vm) for the prepared nanocomposite and other peroxidase-like materials in comparison with HRP.
Comparison of the analytical characteristics of the presented method with some similar works reported in the literature.

row.entry: MoS2 quantum dots / graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of GQD/UiO-66 nanocomposite for fluorometric determination of cholesterol in human serum samples using the developed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations (mmol L–1) ± standard deviation

head.ce:title: A fluorescent biosensor based on graphene quantum dots/zirconium-based metal-organic framework nanocomposite as a peroxidase mimic for cholesterol monitoring in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Spectroscopy Research Lab., Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 km Tabriz-Marageh Road, P.O. Box 53714-161, Tabriz 5375171379, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Spectroscopy Research Lab., Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 km Tabriz-Marageh Road, P.O. Box 53714-161, Tabriz 5375171379, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, graphene quantum dots/zirconium-based metal–organic framework (GQDs/UiO-66) nanocomposite was synthesized via a solvothermal procedure in one pot. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite in peroxidase-like reactions was investigated using colorimetric and spectrofluorometric techniques. The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite in peroxidase mimetic-based fluorescence analysis of H2O2 based on H2O2-terephthalic acid reaction was considerably enhanced in comparison with its components. Owing to the fact that H2O2 could be produced as a result of enzymatic oxidation of cholesterol, and there is a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and cholesterol concentration, a fluorescent biosensor was developed for indirect determination of the cholesterol. In the optimal condition, the calibration plot was linear in the concentration range between 0.04 and 1.60 µmol L−1, with a detection limit of 0.01 µmol L−1. The method validation was checked by analyzing the NIST SRM 1952a-2, as a certified reference material, and the evolved technique was successfully used for cholesterol quantification in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: During recent decades, cost-effective artificial enzymes with high stability have been developed as alternatives for natural ones [1]. The use of distinct nanomaterials having intrinsic peroxidase-like activity has been increasingly developed, and various biosensors have been established using different organic and inorganic materials [2]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) include some synthetic polymeric hybrid materials with metal ions or metal clusters and an organic compound as a linker of different parts of their structure [3]. In addition to their good porosity, MOFs have many other advantages, including good compatibility with organic and aqueous media and low cost, if the applied metal source is abundant [4]. Due to the different properties of MOFs, they have many different applications in various fields such as separating processes [5], purifying systems [6], gas storage [7], drug delivery/storage [8], energy and development of sensors [9] and catalysis [10]. To prepare MOFs with high stability, some metals like Ti and Zr have been used. As they are stable enough, they can be used for catalytic purposes [11]. Abundance and low toxicity of Zr make it a right candidate for application in MOF systems. Zr-MOFs with large porosity and very high stability present an excellent accessibility to the redox sites, which is considered progress in the biomimetic catalytic process [12]. By controlled chemical modifying of the MOFs, they can present special functions, and a new route is opened to design novel generations of sensors [13].

Due to their quantum confinement and edge effects, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) show good surface area, notable chemical, optical and electrical properties, and also high solubility and great biocompatibility. These extraordinary characteristics of GQDs give the potential of being applied in many different fields like catalysis. In this study, the catalytic activity of GQD/UiO-66 nanocomposite (NC) in the cholesterol detection was investigated.

Cholesterol (cholest-5-en-3β-ol) and its related ester derivatives are essential components in the structure of the membrane of all animal cells [14–18]. The normal cholesterol concentration in the blood serum is between 200 and 240 mg dL–1. Many diseases including arteriosclerosis, cerebral thrombosis, heart disease, hypertension, and coronary artery disease are caused by high cholesterol levels in the blood serum, and also low levels of cholesterol lead to anemia and hepatopathy [19]. So, a comprehensive and quick assessment of cholesterol has changed to a universal challenge [20]. So far, several techniques such as chromatographic methods including gas chromatography and high-performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical sensors, and optical sensors especially colorimetric, and fluorescent sensors have been developed for cholesterol assays in biological samples [20–34]. It is well-known that in the presence of oxygen and cholesterol oxidase (ChOx), free cholesterol undergoes oxidation. This reaction leads to the production of 4-cholestene-3-one and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). If the H2O2 level is determined, it can be indirectly related to the cholesterol amount [24]. Owing to the exclusive properties of nanoparticles (NPs), great attention has been recently directed to develop nanomaterials-based optical sensors for cholesterol monitoring in clinical samples. Au NPs [26], ZnO NPs [15], MoS2 nanosheets [27], CuO/graphene nanosphere composite [28], Cu2(OH)3Cl-CeO2 nanocomposite [29], MIL-53(Fe) MOF [30], Cu–hemin MOF [31], bimetallic (Co/2Fe) MOF [32], Ag nanocluster decorated MoS2 nanosheets [33], and mesoporous silica nanoparticle@ZIF-8 MOF nanocomposite [34] have been applied for this purpose.

Among different methods for cholesterol determination, fluorescence is preferred because it is simple, fast, and very sensitive. As well, it has the spatiotemporal resolution, comparatively low-cost, and an extensive linear range [34]. Although the peroxidase mimetic activity of GQDs and photocatalytic property of UiO-66 nanoparticles as a peroxidase mimetic have been individually reported [35,36], to the best of knowledge, there is no report on the application of GQDs/UiO-66 NC as a peroxidase mimetic for cholesterol sensing. Consequently, herein, a fluorescent biosensor based on the GQDs/UiO-66 NC as an innovative peroxidase mimic was used for indirect determination of cholesterol through catalyzing of terephthalic acid (TA) oxidation with H2O2 resulting from cholesterol oxidation in the presence of ChOx. The TA oxidation led to a highly fluorescent product so that the fluorescence intensity was indirectly proportional to the concentration of H2O2 and consequently, the concentration of cholesterol. The prepared new fluorescent biosensor was prosperously applied for monitoring of cholesterol in human serum samples.

For fluorescence analyses, a Perkin Elmer Spectrofluorometer (LS-45, USA) was used. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan) was performed for the absorption experiments. The morphology and size of the prepared nanocomposite was studied by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Mira3-FEG, Czech Republic), and high-resolution transmission electron spectroscope (HRTEM) model JEM-2100F. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by D5000 X-ray diffractometer (Siemens, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) model Tensor 27 (Bruker, Germany) was applied for reporting FT-IR investigations. Porosity and surface area studies of the synthesized materials were done by BELSORP-miniII analyzer (Japan) based on Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) theory using N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms. Solid materials were weighed with a Kern electronic analytical balance model ABT 100–5 M (Germany). The pH adjusting of the solutions was performed using a pH-meter model 827 (Metrohm, Switzerland).

The applied chemicals were analytical grade which was used without any treatment or purification. Zirconium tetrachloride, terephthalic acid (TA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), graphite powder, potassium hydrogen phosphate, HCl, and NaOH were purchased from Merck (Germany). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), cholesterol, cholesterol oxidase (ChOx), and cholesterol esterase (ChE) were obtained from Sigma (USA). Deionized water (DI) obtained from Shahid Ghazi Pharmaceutical (Tabriz, Iran) was used to prepare all solutions. To prepare the cholesterol stock solution, 0.0387 g of its powder along with 1 mL of Triton X-100 was heated in a 100.0 mL volumetric flask at 70° C, and dissolved in DI water (70° C). Then, the solution was stirred for 10 min, and the flask was made up with DI water.

In the present study, the preparation of GQDs was carried out through a hydrothermal process. First, graphene powder was chemically oxidized to graphene oxide by following the modified Hummers method [37]. Then, the obtained graphene oxide was heated with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 in a tubular furnace under the nitrogen atmosphere at 200–300 °C for 2 h. The produced graphene sheets underwent an oxidation process in a mixture of 10 mL concentrated H2SO4 and 30 mL HNO3 for about 15–20 h under moderate ultrasonication (500 W, 40 kHz). Some microporous membranes were applied to purify the oxidized graphene sheets after appropriate dilution, and the purified material was then dispersed in the DI water again. After that, the obtained suspension was conveyed to an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. This process led to the production of a black suspension, which was filtered with a microporous membrane and the brown filtrate was isolated. Finally, the obtained colloidal material was dialyzed using a 3500 Da dialysis bag for about 10 h. The acquired GQDs was stable for over three months [38].

For the synthesis of UiO-66, a previously reported conventional solvothermal method was used [4]. First, 0.053 g of zirconium tetrachloride and 0.034 g of TA were dissolved in 5 mL of DMF as a solvent. Then, the acquired mixture was poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the material was centrifuged and purified with ethanol until the complete removal of occluded DMF molecules. Finally, the white precipitate was dried at 80 °C for 12 h.

A simple one-pot solvothermal method was applied to prepare a series of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposites with different mass ratios of GQDs vs. UiO-66. For this purpose, a specific amount of the synthesized GQDs was added to 10 mL DMF, and sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 60 min. Then, a mixture of 0.053 g zirconium tetrachloride and 0.034 g TA was dissolved in 5 mL DMF under ultrasonication at room temperature. Afterward, the firstly prepared GQDs solution was added to the latter one under continuous ultrasonication for another 1 h. Eventually, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. The final steps were the same as the ones which were done in the case of UiO-66 synthesis.

The following method was applied to detect H2O2 using fluorescence experiments. First, 0.75 mL of 1.0 g L−1 GQDs/UiO-66 (10% w/w) and 0.75 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) were added to aqueous solutions containing H2O2 in the concentration range of 0.5–100.0 nmol L−1. Then, 0.25 mL of 0.02 mol L−1 TA was added to the mixture, and the final volume was reached to 5.0 mL with DI water. After that, the solution was irradiated by a UV lamp (30 W, λ = 254 nm, Philips, the Netherlands) for 10 min under stirring. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 422 nm with excitation at 315 nm.

For cholesterol detection, first, 100 µL of 100 U mL−1 ChOx was added into the standard solutions or serum samples comprising cholesterol in the concentration range of 0.04–1.60 µmol L−1. Then, 300 µL of 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) was added to the solution and incubated for 15 min at room temperature. After that, the procedure was continued as described in the above section.

FE-SEM, TEM, XRD, and FT-IR analyses were done to characterize the synthesized GQD/UiO-66 nanocomposite. For morphology assessment of UiO-66, GQDs and GQDs/UiO-66, TEM, and FE-SEM were used (Fig. 1 a-f). TEM and FE-SEM images of UiO-66 display the octahedral structure with a size around 100 nm and smooth surfaces (Fig. 1a and 1d), which is in good agreement with the previously reported results [4]. The TEM image of the GQDs shows a uniform distribution of the GQDs with a size range lower than 10 nm (Fig. 1e). The size and height profile of the GQDs was also studied by AFM. As can be seen from histogram of Fig. S1, the GQDs diameters are mostly distributed in the range of 2–7 nm, which is in agreement with the TEM results. Moreover, Raman spectroscopic characterization of the GQDs was performed. As shown in Fig. S2, Raman spectrum of the GQDs reveals two characteristic peaks, relating to the D- and G-bands at 1361 and 1582 cm−1, respectively, with an excellent ID/IG ratio of 0.96. These observations show the successful synthesis of the GQDs. The EDX spectrum of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite (Fig. S3) involves C, O and Zr elements, which confirms the formation of UiO-66. Also, the EDX spectrum contains C and O elements, which implies that GQDs are loaded on the UiO-66. The presence of C and O elements are related to both of UiO-66 and GQDs. The FE-SEM micrograph, TEM image, and EDX spectrum of the GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite exhibit that the GQDs have successfully coated on the surface of the UiO-66 MOF.

The XRD patterns of the synthesized UiO-66, GQDs and GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite are represented in Fig. 2 A. As can be seen, the UiO-66 XRD pattern shows two characteristic peaks at 2θ values of 7.35° and 8.7°, and five typical signals positioned at 2θ values of 12.3°, 14.15°, 17.9°, 18.95°, and 19.4° [39]. This XRD pattern is similar to previous reports [40], confirming the successful synthesis of UiO-66. XRD analysis of the GQDs displays a broad diffraction peak (002) at around 18° as a characteristic peak of graphite-like structures. XRD pattern of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite refers to the pristine UiO-66 and GQDs agrees with the previously reported UiO-66, and GQDs XRD patterns [41], confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite.

The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite is compared with the FT-IR spectra of UiO-66 and GQDs in Fig. 2B. The FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized GQDs illustrates vibration peaks at 3433 cm−1, 1587 cm−1, and 1270 cm−1, related to the O–H bond, C = C bond and C–O–H bond, respectively. The observed peaks at 2935 cm−1, and 1056 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of C–H and C–O–C, respectively. The skeletal vibrations of aromatic rings are appeared at around 1587 to 1618 cm−1. The peaks at 1685, 1618, 1423, and 1294 cm−1 are associated with the vibration of C = O bond, aromatic C = C stretching peak, C–O (epoxy) deformation, and stretching vibrations. The FT-IR results indicate that the GQDs have many oxygenated functional groups such as alkoxy, carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups on their surfaces, which make them soluble in water. Some previously reported FT-IR spectra have confirmed these results [42–44]. UiO-66 FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 2B shows two peaks that are associated with symmetric and asymmetric stretching of TA ligand at 1390 and 1571 cm−1. The small bond at 1506 cm−1 is related to the C = C of the benzene ring. The peaks of the C–H and O–H vibrations in the TA ligand are illustrated at 738, 833, 925 cm−1. The asymmetric stretching peak of the Zr-(OC) is illustrated at 565 cm−1. These results are inconsistent with the previous reports [45–47]. The peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite are related to the UiO-66 and GQDs FT-IR peaks, except the additional peaks at 1298, 1519, and 1683 cm−1, which are associated with the C–OH, C = C, and C = O vibrations of the GQD, respectively.

The porosity and surface area of the synthesized materials were studied using N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms based on BET theory (Fig. 2C). As shown in Table S1, the obtained BET surface area for the as-prepared GQDs/UiO-66 is about 885.52184 m2 g−1, which is a little lower than the BET surface area of UiO-66 (1094.28184 m2 g−1). The surface area of the nanocomposite is decreased by applying GQDs in the nanocomposite structure. This decline in the surface area values specifies the loading of GQDs on the UiO-66 matrix during the synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of the GQDs/UiO-66
ce:section.ce:para: With the aim of to assess the peroxidase mimetic activity of the GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite, it was used in a reaction between TA and H2O2. Fig. 3 shows the characteristic fluorescence emission peak of the oxidation product of TA at 422 nm with excitation at 315 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the above-mentioned product in the absence and presence of the GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. 3e, respectively. As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity is negligibly increased in the presence of UiO-66 (Fig. 3c) or GQDs (Fig. 3d). However, the fluorescence intensity is improved more than 8-fold in the presence of the nanocomposite owing to its high affinity to adsorb H2O2 and also synergistic action of its components on the H2O2 dissociation to reactive oxygen species such as •O2 – and •OH [48,49]. This is a sign of the high efficiency of the GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite, showing peroxidase mimetic photocatalytic activity in the GQDs/UiO-66/H2O2/TA system that is necessary for H2O2 measurement.

The impact of experimental conditions including pH, buffer type, and its concentration, reaction duration, the nanocomposite concentration, and weight ratio of its components and TA concentration were studied to optimize the analytical technique. According to Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity is increased by increasing the pH from 3.0 to 6.0, and then reached a plateau from 6.0 to 8.0. By further increasing the pH, the fluorescence intensity is decreased. Thus, pH 7.0 was considered as the optimum pH value. To study the influence of buffer type and its concentration, several buffers such as phosphate, ammonium acetate, and Tris-HCl were examined in the concentration range of 0.002–0.03 mol L−1. According to the results presented in Fig. 4b, 0.015 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution was recommended for further experiments. The effect of TA concentration on the fluorescence intensity was also studied in the concentration range of 0.2–5.0 mmol L−1. The results depicted in Fig. 4c indicate that the highest fluorescence intensity is achieved for 1.0 mmol L−1 TA concentration. Hence, this concentration was selected as an optimum value in further experiments. The effect of incubation time on the photocatalytic efficiency of the GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite under irradiation by a UV lamp was evaluated. As can be seen from Fig. 4d, by increasing the irradiation time up to 10 min, the fluorescence intensity is increased, and then no significant change has occurred. Accordingly, 10 min was used as an optimum incubation time for the rest of the experiments. To investigate the effect of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite concentration on the photocatalytic efficiency, several samples with different weight ratios of GQDs: UiO-66 (5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) were prepared and used in the concentration range of 25–200 mg L−1. Based on the results demonstrated in Fig. 4e, 150 mg L−1 of the nanocomposite with a weight ratio of 10% GQDs: UiO-66 was set as the optimum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2 detection using GQDs/UiO-66 as a peroxidase mimetic
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2 concentration has a considerable effect on the fluorescence intensity relating to the oxidation product attained by the reaction between TA and H2O2 in the presence of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite. A calibration curve was plotted for H2O2 sensing under the optimum conditions; pH; 7.0, incubation (photon irradiation) time; 10 min, GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite concentration; 150 mg L−1, and TA concentration; 1.0 mmol L−1. The results shown in Fig. S4 indicate that the fluorescence intensity is linearly increased in the concentration range between 0.5 and 100.0 nmol L−1 with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.9991. The regression equation is F = 8.746C + 23.264, where C is the H2O2 concentration in nmol L–1, and F is the fluorescence intensity. The detection limit of the method was found to be 0.15 nmol L−1.

The colorimetric experiments were used to investigate the photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/UiO-66 NC in the oxidation process of TMB by H2O2. The high intensity of the produced blue color, leading to the high absorbance of the solution at λmax = 652 nm after oxidation of TMB by H2O2 in the presence of the GQDs/UiO-66 NC, indicates the high performance of the used catalyst (Fig. S5). To study the steady-state kinetics of the GQDs/UiO-66 NC, a set of experiments was accomplished in the case of TMB-H2O2 reaction using the GQDs/UiO-66 NC as a catalyst. In these experiments, all the conditions were unchanged with the except of only one substrate concentration (TMB or H2O2), and the initial reaction rates were fitted to the following Michaelis-Menten equation for both H2O2 and TMB as shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7, respectively.
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V max
×
1
[ S ]
+
1
V max

Where Km, [S], V, and Vmax refer to the Michaelis-Menten constant, reagent concentration, initial and maximum reaction rate, respectively. The Km and Vmax values were calculated for the used catalysts applying the Lineweaver-Burk graphs. The obtained results were compared with the ones acquired for HRP [50] in Table 1 . According to the results shown in Table 1, the GQDs/UiO-66 NC has less Km and higher Vmax values than HRP, indicating its better performance as a peroxidase-like catalyst in TMB-H2O2 reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of the method for cholesterol sensing
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that the primary product of the cholesterol oxidation by ChOx in the presence of oxygen is H2O2. Accordingly, it is possible to detect cholesterol by coupling the presented GQDs/UiO-66-based photocatalytic method and ChOx-based cholesterol oxidation. Following the completion of the enzymatic cholesterol oxidation reaction in a phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), the obtained H2O2 reacted with TA in the presence of GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite as a peroxidase mimic, and the acquired fluorescence intensity was considered as an analytical signal. Consequently, a feasible and straightforward fluorescent biosensor was developed for indirect determination of cholesterol.

The cholesterol calibration curve was constructed by plotting the fluorescence emission intensity versus cholesterol concentration in the range of 0.04–2.00 µmol L–1 under the optimized conditions, and the mean of three curves was assessed for validation studies. In the concentration range of 0.04–1.60 µmol L−1, the presented fluorescence method exhibited a linear response with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.9979 (Fig. S8). The regression equation was F = 571.18C, where C is the cholesterol concentration in μmol L–1, and F is the fluorescence intensity. The detection limit (3Sb /m) and limit of quantification (10Sb /m) of the method were 0.01 and 0.03 µmol L−1, respectively. The intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviations (%RSD, n = 5, cholesterol concentration; 0.20 μmol L−1) were found to be 0.97 and 1.75%, respectively.

These findings were compared against those of other luminescence methods for cholesterol determination in various matrices. The steps for nanostructures’ preparation in the presented method are easy and rapid. Also, the presented method has an excellent potential to be applied for the monitoring of glucose if glucose oxidase is used instead of cholesterol oxidase. As illustrated in Table 2 , the method has better LOD and wider linear range than some other methods. Moreover, the method has some positive aspects such as simplicity, high sensitivity, no need for large consumption of organic solvents, and low cost, making it a suitable analytical method for cholesterol determination in biological samples.

To examine the selectivity of the presented method, the effect of several interfering species that may exist in the real samples on the analytical signal was studied. For this purpose, a constant concentration of cholesterol (0.80 µmol L−1) was analyzed in the presence of numerous interfering species such as uric acid, urea, histidine, glycine, serine, cysteine, vitamin C, fructose, and glucose, that may exist in the human serum samples, under the general procedure. The tolerance limit was considered the maximum concentration causing less than ± 5% variation in the biosensor response. As can be seen from Fig. S9, in the tolerance limit of 1000, the presence of potentially interfering species does not have a striking influence upon the detection of cholesterol by the presented method, indicating the remarkable selectivity of the method for cholesterol detection.

The applicability of the method was inspected through applying it for determining free cholesterol levels in several human serum samples gained from Plasma Pathobiology Lab. (Tabriz, Iran). Each sample was poured into micro tubes and kept at –4 °C up to be analyzed. The frozen human serum samples were thawed at 25 °C and spun to reach proper homogeneity. One milliliter from each serum sample was taken, and 100 µL of 100 U m L−1 ChE was added for complete conversion of the existed cholesterol esters in the serum sample to cholesterol form. After proper dilution with phosphate buffer, the sample was analyzed according to the general procedure, and each measurement was repeated three times. The results are tabulated in Table 3 . The accuracy of the method was evaluated via spiking definite quantities of cholesterol to the serum samples. The relative recovery values were found in the range of 97.67–102.00%, with acceptable RSD% values, revealing high reliability of the method for cholesterol sensing in real samples. More effectiveness for the suggested method was accomplished by analyzing the standard reference material IST SRM 1952a-2. As can be from Table 3, the gained result (5.83 ± 1.11 mmol L–1) is harmonious with the certified cholesterol concentration (5.93 ± 0.10 mmol L–1), confirming the excellent accuracy of the presented method.

The synthesized GQDs/UiO-66 nanocomposite was introduced as a novel peroxidase mimetic nano-catalyst for H2O2 reactions. Fluorometric and colorimetric techniques were used to evaluate the catalyst efficiency. The nanocomposite was used to determine H2O2 based on the H2O2-TA reaction, leading to a highly fluorescent product. Accordingly, a feasible fluorescent biosensor was introduced for indirect detection of cholesterol by the combination of ChOx-based cholesterol oxidation and the presented GQDs/UiO-66-based photocatalytic method. Under the optimized conditions, the calibration plot was linear in the concentration range of 0.04–1.60 µmol L−1. The detection limit was gained as 0.01 µmol L−1 comparable or lower than the similar methods catalyzed by other nanomaterials. The method validation was performed by analyzing the standard reference material NIST SRM 1952a-2, and the developed method was tested on human serum samples as well and some satisfactory results were gained. Accordingly, GQDs/UiO-66/H2O2/TA system owns many advantages, such as considerable sensitivity and selectivity, simplicity of operation, as well as low cost. By using this system, it is possible to perform quantitatively accurate analysis in biomedical diagnoses.

Hossein Abdolmohammad-Zadeh: Resources, Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Farzaneh Ahmadian: Methodology, Software, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of the research council of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant no. ASMU/99372-20) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
